next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

The laundromat on Alder was hotter than her apartment and louder than the committee room had been, which made it easier. Heat rolled off the dryer banks in a soapy, metallic breath and turned the windows into sweating mirrors. Every pair of boots on the floor carried a white salt tide at the edges. Leela sat on an orange plastic chair with her knees touching the black laundry bag and watched her sheets slap the glass as if the machine were trying to shake an answer out of them.

She had brought the good blouse from the meeting too, because it still smelled faintly of the faculty conference room: burnt coffee, radiator dust, the sweet chemical wipe somebody had used on the table before the ambush. She had worn the blouse because it still fit under the blazer she bought for Baltimore and because somewhere in her body a childish part of her believed a committee could be managed by collars. Now the collar points knocked uselessly against the side of the washer. Her defense had not been denied. It had been "postponed indefinitely pending further revision and review," which was worse in the particular way academic language liked to be worse. Denial had a date inside it. Indefinitely was a hallway with fluorescent lighting.

She unlocked her phone to check the time and saw the Hudson Federal reminder at the top of the screen again: minimum payment due on the eighteenth. Under it sat Mohit's email from 7:12 that morning, subject line "Next Steps." He had written three short paragraphs in the careful managerial tone he used when he wanted cruelty to look like structure...
open_hooks_to_carry: Keep the room small and procedural so the cruelty feels fully respectable.
Show how prior chapters' bureaucratic consequences now combine into a devastating narrative against Leela.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...r phone one last time.

There was a new message, but not from June.

From Ben: `He said not to worry if people seem weird. He said these things get clarified. I know you probably don't want updates. Sorry.`

No question mark. No request for forgiveness. Even shame had not broken his habit of reporting upward and sideways at once.

Leela stared at the text until the screen dimmed.

Then she opened the thread with June and saw only her own messages, stacked one over the other like notes slid under a locked door. The last thing in the conversation from June was three nights earlier, before Ben, before Mohit's email, before this new clean split in the air between people still speaking and people managing. It was about nothing. Eggs. Did Leela need eggs. June had been at the store.

Leela sat on the edge of the bed in her coat. The damp sheet in the basket cooled against her shin. Somewhere down the hall, somebody laughed at a television laugh track, huge and canned and oblivious. Her radiator kept striking the same two notes.

Mohit had moved first. That was the simple version. The truer one was worse: he had moved into openings they had made for him, and now the only coalition that had felt briefly larger than his office existed mostly as separate silences. Omar protecting paperwork. June protecting a future she could still imagine. Ben protecting the part of himself that still believed proximity to power could be mistaken for safety.

Leela put the phone face down on the mattress and listened to the pipes hammer through the wall like a building trying to warn itself too late.
state_deltas_required: Leela needs her pre-defense committee meeting to end with approval to schedule the final dissertation defense.
Her defense is postponed indefinitely, collapsing the future she has organized her adult life around.
